claim: Vaccinating the gastrointestinal tract is not effective in protecting rectal and vaginal mucosa.

result.claim: Vaccinating the gastrointestinal tract is not effective in protecting rectal and vaginal mucosa.
result.reasoning: The claim is supported by evidence from a review paper (0664ee10a6957dea1e11e036ecaa12ab5844d53e) which explicitly states that "Oral administration does not offer protection in the genital tract as it induces only a restricted response (gut, salivary glands, and mammary glands)" (Proposition 213138). This directly addresses the ineffectiveness of gastrointestinal (oral) vaccination in protecting vaginal mucosa (part of the genital tract). While the paper also notes that "Vaginal and rectal vaccine delivery are not currently used partially due to the restricted mucosal response to the inductor site" (Proposition 213136), this highlights the general challenge of inducing broad mucosal immunity from a single site, further supporting the idea that a distant site like the GI tract would not effectively protect rectal and vaginal mucosa. Other propositions discuss oral vaccination for general mucosal immunity or local delivery to rectal/vaginal tissues, but do not contradict the specific claim about GI tract vaccination's ineffectiveness in protecting distant rectal and vaginal mucosa.

evidence.text: The drug delivery platform is able to be administered into the vaginal and rectal mucosal sites.
evidence.source: Efficacy of silk fibroin biomaterial vehicle for in vivo mucosal delivery of Griffithsin and protection against HIV and SHIV infection ex vivo.

evidence.text: SF and SF-Grft placement in the rectal tract exhibited no changes in cytokine production.
evidence.source: Efficacy of silk fibroin biomaterial vehicle for in vivo mucosal delivery of Griffithsin and protection against HIV and SHIV infection ex vivo.

evidence.text: Immunizing with vaccines that induce immune responses in mucosal gut tissue could improve long-term treatment of HIV infection in humans.
evidence.source: Therapeutic DNA vaccine induces broad T cell responses in the gut and sustained protection from viral rebound and AIDS in SIV-infected rhesus macaques.

evidence.text: Multiple genera within Bacteroidetes were significantly decreased in the rectal microbiome following immunization, including Prevotella, Alloprevotella, Bacteroides, Acetobacteroides, Falsiporphyromonas, and Anaerocella.
evidence.source: Rectal Microbiome Composition Correlates with Humoral Immunity to HIV-1 in Vaccinated Rhesus Macaques.

evidence.text: Immunizing with vaccines that induce immune responses in mucosal gut tissue could reduce residual viral reservoirs during drug therapy.
evidence.source: Therapeutic DNA vaccine induces broad T cell responses in the gut and sustained protection from viral rebound and AIDS in SIV-infected rhesus macaques.

evidence.text: The rectal and cervico-vaginal mucosae are common routes of transmission of HIV in man.
evidence.source: Mucosal infection and vaccination against feline immunodeficiency virus.

evidence.text: It is possible to reliably infect cats via the rectal and vaginal routes.
evidence.source: Mucosal infection and vaccination against feline immunodeficiency virus.

evidence.text: CT is more effective when used as an adjuvant via the intra-nasal than the rectal route.
evidence.source: Mucosal infection and vaccination against feline immunodeficiency virus.

evidence.text: The oral mucosa is a site for mucosal vaccination.
evidence.source: HIV-1 vaccination by needle-free oral injection induces strong mucosal immunity and protects against SHIV challenge

evidence.text: TLN vaccination was more effective than rectal immunisation in stimulating both humoral responses and proliferative responses in cats.
evidence.source: Mucosal infection and vaccination against feline immunodeficiency virus.

evidence.text: Oral vaccination is a potential option to elicit systemic and mucosal immunity against HIV.
evidence.source: HIV-1 vaccination by needle-free oral injection induces strong mucosal immunity and protects against SHIV challenge

evidence.text: SF and Grft did not alter the microbiome in the rectal tract.
evidence.source: Efficacy of silk fibroin biomaterial vehicle for in vivo mucosal delivery of Griffithsin and protection against HIV and SHIV infection ex vivo.

evidence.text: SF and SF-Grft placement in the rectal tract exhibited no changes in cytokine production.
evidence.source: Efficacy of silk fibroin biomaterial vehicle for in vivo mucosal delivery of Griffithsin and protection against HIV and SHIV infection ex vivo.

evidence.text: Several mucosal administration strategies have been explored, such as vaginal, rectal, oral, nasal, or sublingual (SL).
evidence.source: Sublingual antigen delivery: a solution for needle-free HIV vaccination

evidence.text: Oral administration does not offer protection in the genital tract.
evidence.source: Sublingual antigen delivery: a solution for needle-free HIV vaccination

evidence.text: Vaginal and rectal vaccine delivery are not currently used partially due to the restricted mucosal response to the inductor site.
evidence.source: Sublingual antigen delivery: a solution for needle-free HIV vaccination

evidence.text: Effective Grft release and retention in mucosal tissues from the SF‐Grft platform resulted in protection against SHIV challenge in both rhesus macaque vaginal and rectal tissues.
evidence.source: Efficacy of silk fibroin biomaterial vehicle for in vivo mucosal delivery of Griffithsin and protection against HIV and SHIV infection ex vivo.

evidence.text: The rectal microbiome exhibited an increase in the ratio of Firmicutes to Bacteroidetes following immunization.
evidence.source: Rectal Microbiome Composition Correlates with Humoral Immunity to HIV-1 in Vaccinated Rhesus Macaques.

evidence.text: Effective Grft release and retention in mucosal tissues from the SF-Grft platform resulted in protection against SHIV challenge in both rhesus macaque vaginal and rectal tissues.
evidence.source: Efficacy of silk fibroin biomaterial vehicle for in vivo mucosal delivery of Griffithsin and protection against HIV and SHIV infection ex vivo.

evidence.text: The rectal microbiome composition can influence HIV-1 vaccine immunogenicity in rhesus macaques.
evidence.source: Rectal Microbiome Composition Correlates with Humoral Immunity to HIV-1 in Vaccinated Rhesus Macaques.
